data.title: GOP senator, wife read mean tweets: ‘Bet you were bullied in middle school’
data.description:
Sen. Eric Schmitt (R-Mo.) tried to get into the Christmas spirit on X, the often acerbic social media platform, by posting a video of himself and his wife, Jaime, reading aloud the put-downs aimed at him online.

The harshest tweet he got was from mka5211 who blasted his “white man entitlement,” “hideously immoral ego” and “shallow soul.”

Check yourself Eric, bet you were bullied in middle school,” the commenter advised.

Schmitt seemed to relish in some of the social media attacks as he and his wife read them out to their online followers.

“My lovely wife Jaime is in town this week so we thought we’d celebrate the holiday season by reading mean tweets,” Schmitt explained the top of the clip.  

Soft jazz played in the background of the video as the senator ticked through the nasty comments. 

Some of the online barbs were too inappropriate for Jaime to read on video, such as a blast from a commenter who called the Missouri senator a “steaming pile of Schmitt.”

Another critic lambasted Schmitt over his defense of the Trump administration’s strikes on Venezuelan boats.

“Venezuela is not a Narco State you dinosaur brained hick,” one person posted.

That one got a big laugh out of Schmitt and a chuckles from his wife.

The one comment that seemed to get under the senator’s skin, however, was a post that declared Schmitt is a “disgrace” to St. Louis and the Cardinals, the city’s baseball team.

“Those are fighting words,” Schmitt replied, pointing at the camera.

Another critic, Chuck265, complained that Schmitt is “on Fox News more than their anchors.”

“Please tell me something he’s accomplished other than hawking his book and playing congressional baseball,” the person grumbled, prompting another laugh from the senator.

Schmitt also read out some of the compliments he got.

One fan wrote: “Eric Schmitt, I voted for you and you’ve not let me down.”

“Thank  you, I’ll never let you down,” Schmitt responded, looking tenderly into the camera.

data.title: Trump’s strategy document: Not as isolationist as you expected
data.description:
Apart from Nicolas Maduro, who already faces a major American military buildup off his shores, Europeans seem most distressed by the new National Security Strategy.

The document’s opening two pages have garnered the most attention, especially abroad, and most especially in Europe. They closely reflect not only President Trump’s views, but also what writers call his “voice” — so much that they might well have been dictated by the man himself. “Over the past nine months,” the very first sentence asserts, “we have brought our nation — and the world — back from the brink of catastrophe and disaster.” This sounds like a perfect rendition of the president’s self-image.

Meanwhile, getting “radical gender ideology and woke lunacy out of our Armed Forces,” is a recurring theme in many of the president’s speeches and musings. Not everyone  will agree that he has “rebuilt our alliances.” Nor will all subscribe to his claim that “over the course of just eight months, we settled eight raging conflicts,” the basis for his quest for the Nobel Peace Prize. But such sentiments have generated apoplectic reactions not only from those Americans who cannot fathom anything that bears a White House imprint, but also and especially from European politicians, journalists and other members of the continent’s elites.

A careful look at what follows the president’s preface yields a somewhat different impression, however. No doubt the four-page section on the Western Hemisphere, headlined “The Trump Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine,” marks a major sea change in America’s traditional national security priorities, which by definition relegates Europe to a lower rung.

While Argentina, the beneficiary of an American financial bailout, will likely welcome Trump’s emphasis on the Americas, it will certainly find no welcome in Caracas and the capitals of other left leaning states, notably Mexico, Colombia and Brazil. Other countries in the Hemisphere, even those friendlier to Washington, might nevertheless resent the “gringos” attempting once again to dominate them.

On the other hand, Europeans, despite their initial panic, might have less cause for concern. Both the National Security Strategy and Secretary of War Pete Hegseth’s subsequent remarks to the Reagan National Defense Forum make clear that the U.S. remains committed to supporting its allies. There is a proviso, however, which Hegseth stated openly at the forum: Washington is only prepared to fight alongside allies who show they take their own defense seriously by substantially increasing their defense expenditures. He went beyond generalizations, identifying “model allies that step up,” including Israel, South Korea, Poland, “increasingly Germany,” the Baltics and the Nordic countries, which “will receive our special favor.” He demanded “partnerships not dependencies.” It is not an unreasonable request. 

Neither Hegseth nor the National Security Strategy even hint at America’s dropping out of NATO, whose support among the U.S. public is 68 percent, according to the Reagan National Defense Survey. Seventy-six percent support an American military response if a NATO ally comes under attack. 

Certainly, Europeans are disconcerted by the strategy’s call for Europe to stem its “civilizational erasure.” Yet however much Trump wishes to see a more rightward tilt in European politics, he has little leverage to make that happen.

Ukrainians have been especially troubled by the strategy document’s seemingly benign words about Russia and the absence of language calling out Moscow as an aggressor. Then again, there are limits to any mollycoddling of Vladimir Putin, as evinced by the outcry that led to the reduction to 19 points from the original 28 point peace plan that Russia appears to have co-authored or even authored.

In the face of rants from the isolationist right, the Reagan Forum poll indicates fully 45 percent of Americans support Ukraine’s full territorial liberation. Another 23 percent are willing to contemplate some Ukrainian territorial concessions, but only in exchange for both peace and security guarantees.

It is noteworthy that allies elsewhere in the world have not been as vocal — and with good reason. They welcome statements like Hegseth’s, which argue that America must deal with China in terms of “deterrence by denial,” which America can only achieve by means of an ongoing buildup of its forces in the Western Pacific. Moreover, East Asian allies and partners have long balanced security concerns with trade with their huge neighbor, a posture that the Trump administration’s strategy echoes.

Taiwan also should have few quibbles with it. The document assigns priority to “deterring a conflict over Taiwan, ideally by preserving military overmatch.” Moreover, it assures that “we will maintain our longstanding declaratory policy on Taiwan, meaning that the United States does not support any unilateral change to the status quo in the Taiwan Strait.”  Every administration in recent decades has articulated an identical policy.

Finally, the strategy document rightly celebrates American successes in the Middle East. The Abraham Accords remain viable. The Gaza ceasefire continues to hold, however unsteadily. All but one of the Israeli hostages have been released, either to their families or for proper burial. Trump asserts that Phase Two of the Gaza ceasefire is imminent. Finally, there is no denying that the Twelve-Day War with Iran weakened its nuclear program and sent Tehran a message that Israel and America are prepared to strike again if necessary.

There is much to challenge in the strategy, but it makes clear that Trump is no isolationist. But such documents have very short-half lives. National security affairs are subject to black swan events, at which point even the best of strategy documents is quickly consigned to oblivion.

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: Amanda Seyfried says she won’t apologize over Charlie Kirk comments
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Actress Amanda Seyfried is doubling down on comments she made after the assassination of conservative political activist Charlie Kirk.

“I’m not f—ing apologizing for that,” Seyfried, 40, told Who What Wear in an interview published Wednesday. “I mean, for f—’s sake, I commented on one thing. I said something that was based on actual reality and actual footage and actual quotes.”

She added, “What I said was pretty damn factual, and I’m free to have an opinion, of course.”

In September, Seyfried faced backlash over an Instagram comment posted shortly after Kirk’s death, writing at the time, “He was hateful.”

The actress told Who What Wear that she considered deleting the post but decided to keep it up, despite the social media backlash. Instead, she made another post to clarify her comment.

“We’re forgetting the nuance of humanity,” Seyfried wrote in September. “I can get angry about misogyny and racist rhetoric and ALSO very much agree that Charlie Kirk’s murder was absolutely disturbing and deplorable in every way imaginable.”

“No one should have to experience this level of violence,” she continued. “This country is grieving too many senseless and violent deaths and shootings. Can we agree on that at least?”

Kirk, founder of Turning Point USA (TPUSA) and an influential ally of President Trump, was fatally shot on Sept. 10 while speaking at an event at Utah Valley University.

data.title: Indiana governor says he’ll help primary Republicans who defied Trump on redistricting
data.description:
Indiana Gov. Mike Braun (R ) on Thursday said he would help President Trump primary Republican state lawmakers who voted against a redistricting measure backed by the leader.

“I am very disappointed that a small group of misguided State Senators have partnered with Democrats to reject this opportunity to protect Hoosiers with fair maps and to reject the leadership of President Trump. Ultimately, decisions like this carry political consequences,” Braun wrote in a post on the social platform X.

‘I will be working with the President to challenge these people who do not represent the best interests of Hoosiers,” he added.

In a 19-31 vote, the Indiana Senate rejected the redistricting bid, with more Republicans voting against the new map than for it.

“We’ll be launching primary challenges against every last traitor who voted no, effective immediately! Pack your bags, your time is up!” Trump adviser Alex Bruesewitz wrote on the social platform X, responding to the vote.

Republicans lawmakers in the Hoosier State who opposed the effort have defended their decision to buck Trump.

“Make no mistake, I, like many of those who will join me in voting no today, are constitutional fiscal and religious conservatives. What that means to me is that I believe in conserving the values, the culture and the institutions that created American exceptionalism,” said Indiana state Sen. Spencer Deery (R), who voted against the map.

“My point is that my opposition to mid-cycle gerrymandering is not in contrast with my conservative principles,” he said. “My opposition is driven by them.”

Trump has threatened to primary all GOP lawmakers that defied his mid-decade redistricting push in the state.

“They found some Republican ‘SUCKERS,’ and they couldn’t be happier that they did! Guys like Failed Senate Candidate Mitch Daniels, who I opposed in his Race against Senator Jim Banks, and Cam Savage, whoever that is, are fighting against the Republican Party, all the way,” Trump wrote in a lengthy Truth Social post ahead of Thursday’s vote.

He specifically slammed Indiana State Senate Leader Rod Bray (R ). 

“Bray and his friends are the favorite Republicans of [House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries [D-N.Y.], Crazy [Rep.] Nancy Pelosi [D-Calif.], and Cryin’ Chuck Schumer [D-N.Y.]. Anybody that votes against Redistricting, and the SUCCESS of the Republican Party in D.C., will be, I am sure, met with a MAGA Primary in the Spring,” he wrote.

data.title: Live updates: Trump, Braun target Indiana’s defiant GOP lawmakers; Venezuela moves raise alarm
data.description:
As the 2026 midterms quickly approach, Republicans are feeling increasingly uneasy over recent election outcomes.

The latest blow came Thursday, after Indiana Republicans bucked Trump’s demand that the state redraw its maps to get two more congressional districts into the fold.

Trump and Indiana Gov. Mike Braun (R) both ripped into the group of GOP state senators who defied them. “I will be working with the President to challenge these people who do not represent the best interests of Hoosiers,” Braun said on social platform X.

Mixed into that discontent is a struggle in Washington to confront the expiration of Affordable Care Act subsidies and a subsequent rise in health care premiums, along with growing concern over Democrats winning the argument on affordability.

Much of Washington’s attention is also on Venezuela, where Trump has upped pressure on President Nicolás Maduro after he announced the seizure of an oil tanker earlier this week. The administration on Thursday issued sanctions on some of the president’s family members and six Venezuelan shipping companies.

Democrats are increasingly alarmed by the moves.

Trump on Friday will swear-in the leader of the Export-Import Bank and will sign bills. The House is in briefly today, and the Senate is out.

Follow along live below.

data.title: In Syria, relief and reality collide one year after Assad’s fall
data.description:
ALEPPO, SYRIA — Early Monday morning in Aleppo, soldiers lined the streets near the old city walls, standing in loose formation beside battered buildings as men gathered quietly around them. Almost no women appeared without head coverings — a reminder of the city’s conservative character even on a day of national celebration.

The mood was subdued at first, as police paced the sidewalks in anticipation of the anniversary parade. By afternoon, the stillness had given way to a noisy procession of cars and trucks draped in Syrian flags, horns blaring under a gray sky.

The joy was real. Aleppo, once the city most devastated in Syria’s civil war, still wears the scars more visibly than most Syrian cities. A year after interim President Ahmed al-Sharaa’s forces swept unexpectedly into Damascus and toppled longtime dictator Bashar al-Assad, many Syrians here seemed grateful for even a fleeting reason to cheer.

But the outward joy masks a far more unsettled reality.

Sharaa, a former al-Qaeda commander turned head-of-state, has secured diplomatic victories that eluded Assad for decades. Washington and Gulf capitals now treat him as Syria’s best chance at stability. Sanctions are loosening; foreign executives shuttle in and out, signing contracts. The government speaks confidently about reopening Syria to the world.

But the political transition has also opened the country’s deepest wounds, and Sharaa’spledges to govern “for all Syrians” have yet to convince those who fear his past or have suffered at the hands of his security forces.

Sharaa’s critics point to October’s parliamentary elections as another letdown. There was no direct popular vote. Most seats were chosen through electoral colleges, with Sharaa appointing the rest. When only a handful of minority candidates won seats, even the election committee admitted “serious shortcomings.”

Nowhere is that distrust sharper than among Syria’s minorities, including Alawites, Christians, Druze and Kurds, who survived the Assad years only to face new threats. Sectarian atrocities over the last year have killed thousands.

In March, more than a thousand Alawite civilians — members of the Islamic sect associated with Assad and resented by some for perceived privilege under his rule — were massacred along the coast. In June, a jihadist opened fire and detonated explosives inside Damascus’s Greek Orthodox Mar Elias Church, killing at least 30 worshippers. And in July, clashes in Suwayda between Druze groups and Bedouin tribes drew in government forces, who carried out on-the-spot executions of Druze men and other abuses.

During ten days traveling through Damascus, Tartous, Latakia, and Aleppo, photographer and filmmaker Jordan Allott (my brother) and I met survivors and relatives of victims from each of these communities. All expressed the same three things: fear, skepticism that this government can unite the country, and deep uncertainty about whether Syria can be held together at all.

Sharaa may be persuading foreign governments, but at home the doubts are more entrenched. “I wouldn’t trust a former terrorist,” a young Druze woman in Damascus told me. “A terrorist will always be a terrorist.” Many minorities believe outsiders are being taken in by a man who merely traded combat fatigues for a Western suit.

The unease isn’t confined to minorities, either. In Damascus, a young Sunni woman guiding us through the city noted how liquor stores in Christian districts had shuttered, and how crowds that once stayed out late now thin out quickly after dark. The reason, she and others said, is straightforward: A former jihadist and his cadre are now in charge, and many Syrians, including some Sunnis, are adjusting their behavior to avoid drawing attention.

A Druze journalist in Damascus told me that although people can criticize the government more openly than before, the loosening of restrictions has also encouraged a rise in sectarian intimidation — mostly Sunni extremists warning minorities that “their time is coming,” as he put it.

Infrastructure reflects the same fragility. Sharaa’s government purged many local administrators who worked under Assad, and in countless places their positions have not been filled. Across much of the country, it is hard to look in any direction and not see trash piled in streets and vacant lots — a reminder that dismantling a regime is far easier than building a functioning state.

Almost everywhere we traveled, we met people preparing to leave. One family was planning to emigrate after their son was kidnapped — targeted, they believe, because he was Alawite. A Druze woman pursuing a master’s degree in Europe saw education as her only viable path out. We also met more than a dozen Alawites who had already crossed into Lebanon. They are living difficult lives there under a government that would prefer fewer Syrians, but they still said they felt safer there than at home.

The cheers and horns faded as we turned into the nearby Christian quarter, where a large Christmas tree stood in a small plaza and women walked uncovered. We stepped into Warsha, a small film company where a group of young Syrians were racing to finish a short film about women’s trauma — a subject no one in this country needs explained. The contrast was striking: outside, flag-waving crowds; inside, students and volunteers hunched over laptops and cameras, trying to meet a deadline.

There I met Sarah, a 19-year-old Christian engineering student from Suwayda who now studies in Aleppo. Her year had been split between the two cities, giving her a view of what she called “many Syrias, each with its own reality.” She spoke of sectarian tensions, pressure to alter how she dressed, and her sense that nothing in Syria feels predictable. “We are not in a stable country,” she said. “We are not free yet.”

Yet Sarah is one of the few young people we met who wants to stay. Her family hopes to emigrate, but she dreams of remaining in Syria — even, someday, of helping lead it. “If all the good people leave, there will be no Syria left,” she told me.

A year after Assad’s fall, Syrians are still weighing relief against reality. The celebrations in Aleppo were genuine. But the country’s future may depend less on those who cheered that day than on the rare young Syrians who, like Sarah, believe there is still something here worth building.

Daniel Allott is the former opinion editor at The Hill and the author of “On the Road in Trump’s America: A Journey into the Heart of a Divided Country.”

data.title: Drunk raccoon who ransacked Virginia liquor store is a serial break-in artist
data.description:
HANOVER COUNTY, Va. (WRIC) – The now-infamous raccoon who got drunk after breaking into a liquor store in Hanover County, Va., is apparently a serial offender.

The story of a raccoon busting his way into state Alcoholic Beverage Control (ABC) outlet, ransacking its shelves and passing out drunk in its bathroom on Nov. 28 has garnered national attention. It’s inspired Virginia ABC advertisements, themed cocktail recipes, a mass scavenger hunt and even a pet election.

Most of all, a limited-time merchandise campaign has raised over $200,000 for the Hanover County Animal Protection Shelter as of the time of reporting.

But the saga isn’t over yet. On Thursday, Dec. 11, Hanover County released an episode of its podcast “Hear In Hanover,” which featured an interview with Animal Protection Officer Samantha Martin — the person who responded to this head-scratching call the morning of Saturday, Nov. 29.

During this interview, she shared new details about the furry criminal.

“This is not the first time he’s been in one of the buildings,” Martin said, referring to the strip mall in which the ABC store is located. “Supposedly, this is the third break-in he’s had. He was in the karate studio — I think he got into the DMV [and] ate some of their snacks one time.”

The animal protection officer added that she took the raccoon back to the shelter and let him rest in a dog kennel out back for a few hours. She emphasized how important it was to keep him isolated from staff and volunteers because, if he accidentally broke someone’s skin, the shelter would have to perform a rabies test on him — and rabies tests can only be performed if an animal is euthanized.

“For his safety, I was dead set — ‘Do not touch this animal. We need him back into the wild,'” Martin said. “Because he didn’t do anything wrong. He was just having a good time!”

Once the raccoon finished sleeping off his bad decisions, he was released back into the area surrounding the strip mall. But why would authorities return him to the scene of his apparent crime spree?

“We don’t relocate him, because that’s a death sentence for raccoons,” Martin explained. “But somehow he knows how to get back in this building. He’s a smart little critter!”

She emphasized that she knows this trashed panda will be back, but she hopes he’ll maybe know better and drink in moderation next time.

“I hope he learned his lesson,” Martin continued. “But I just say, enjoy your life. I think he’s living his best life – and why not? Have a drink or two, especially on Black Friday!”

She added that no one at the Hanover County Animal Protection Shelter thought this story would go as far as it did. Martin admitted that she hadn’t even wanted to post the photos at first, as she wasn’t confident that anyone would find the caper funny. She said the fact that it ended up on SNL was “crazy” — but in the best way.

The animal control official noted that it’s been wonderful to see people come together and laugh, especially during stressful times.

“I think people can see the human side of it — and everybody’s been there,” Martin said. “Everybody’s had a few extra and passed out by the toilet and hoped somebody can come and help you the next morning.”

More than anything, Martin said she’s been so happy to see how much money this has raised for the shelter.

“We desperately need it [the money] because our shelter is extremely old,” she said. “And the county is growing like wildfire; we can’t keep up. We need more kennel space, we need a bigger well … we just need a bigger shelter, for the size of the county.”

The company that’s producing the charitable merchandise, Bonfire, actually reached out to the shelter with the idea. Again, shelter staff never thought “Trashed Panda” shirts, hoodies and more would be as successful as they have been, according to Martin.

She admitted that many of them just liked the idea of having shirts for themselves, which Bonfire was happy to provide — but then this campaign, much like this story itself, took on a life of its own.

These limited-time items, as of the time of reporting, will only be available for a few more days. You can browse all of the Trashed Panda merchandise here.

The funds raised through these sales will hopefully pay for a new well or an addition onto the shelter, Martin said.

“You have no idea what you have done,” she said, when asked what she’d tell those who have contributed. “We are so, so grateful. The animals, truly, are blessed — and we do try our very best to save [every animal].”

data.title: Trump signals willingness to work with Democrats on health care
data.description:
President Trump suggested Thursday that he would be willing to work with Democrats on the issue of health care, as concerns over rising insurance premiums grow.

“What a nice group of Democrats,” Trump said, speaking to the crowd at the White House Congressional Ball. “We have a lot of Democrats and we welcome you. Honestly, we do.” 

“I think we’re going to start working together on health care,” he continued “I really predict that.”

Trump’s comments come as Republican leadership on Capitol Hill find themselves facing growing pressure to address affordability issues — including health care costs.

On Thursday, the Senate failed to advance competing GOP and Democratic health proposals, raising the risk of 2021 subsidies under the Affordable Care Act expiring at the end of the year. The enhanced tax credits have helped to keep insurance costs lower for struggling Americans.

The expected lapse of the subsidies, a major issue during the 43-day government shutdown, signals another fight over health care could be imminent in the new year.

Trump, himself, has said he likes the idea of direct payments to health savings accounts — a proposal put forward by the GOP bill — for Americans as a solution.

data.title: Court blocks immigration officials from re-detaining Abrego Garcia
data.description:
A federal judge blocked Immigrations and Customs Enforcement (ICE) from re-detaining mistakenly deported man Kilmar Abrego Garcia during his ICE appointment Friday morning.

A day earlier, U.S. District Judge Paula Xinis ordered ICE to immediately release Abrego Garcia, finding it had no lawful authority to detain him.

Abrego Garcia was released from immigration custody Thursday evening, and he was given an ICE supervision order that sets various conditions and requires him to return for an appointment Friday at 8 a.m. EST in Baltimore.

Abrego Garcia’s lawyers rushed back to Xinis’s court, warning the Trump administration would attempt to re-detain him.  They also raised concern about the impact of an immigration judge’s ruling in his case that came down later Thursday evening.

“For the public to have any faith in the orderly administration of justice, the Court’s narrowly crafted remedy cannot be so quickly and easily upended without further briefing and consideration,” Xinis wrote.

DEVELOPING

data.title: Jeffries on Indiana redistricting vote failure; ‘A good night for the American people’
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) on Thursday praised Indiana Senate Republicans for striking down redistricting efforts in the state.

“Well, across the board, it was a good night for the American people and a bad day for Donald Trump,” Jeffries said during an appearance on MS Now’s “The Briefing.” 

“And in Indiana, his effort to try to rig the midterm elections, because he knows that his policies has been the disaster, the Republican Party has been a disaster, the House Republican Conference has been a disaster, they’ve got nothing positive to present to the American people,” the New York Democrat told host Jen Psaki.

The Hoosier State Senate rejected a new GOP-favored House map in a 19-31 vote. The lower chamber passed the measure, which was backed by President Trump, last Friday — just days after the new district lines were proposed.

The president was quick to slam state Senate GOP Leader Rodric Bray (R) for not backing the new district lines. 

“I heard he was against it,” Trump told reporters in the Oval Office, when asked about the upper chamber’s rejection of the map. “He’ll probably lose his next primary whenever that is. I hope he does because he’s done a tremendous disservice.”

Trump has in recent days ramped up his threats to support primary challenges to Republicans that push back on redistricting.

Jeffries said the vote against Trump’s recommendation signals a change in the president’s base, adding that Republicans are “trying to gerrymander congressional maps all across the country.  

“And that’s not working for them,” the lawmaker continued. “And they didn’t expect the forceful Democratic response, which we’ve seen, of course, in California, we’ve seen movement in Virginia and other states, because we’ve made clear from the very beginning, we will not let these Republicans mathematically gerrymander their way into artificially keeping their majority.”

In recent months, a number of Democratic and Republican states have voted to introduce mid-decade redistricting ahead of next year’s elections. Among them are Texas, North Carolina, California, Missouri, Ohio and Utah — with several sparking legal battles.
